macro_segments.title: Introduction to Shomeret Yavam: The Status of a Sister-in-Law Awaiting Levirate Marriage

micro_segments.title: Opening remarks and the unusual cases discussed in the perek
The role of the yavam: levirate marriage as carryover from the deceased brother's marriage
Whether the yavam can participate in hafarat nedarim alongside the father

macro_segments.title: The Mishnah: Single vs. Multiple Brothers-in-Law and the Dispute of Rabbi Eliezer, Rabbi Yehoshua, and Rabbi Akiva

micro_segments.title: Recitation and basic setup of the Mishnah: shomeret yavam with one or two brothers
Rabbi Eliezer's position: the yavam can be mefir regardless of number
Rabbi Yehoshua's position: only if there is one yavam, not two
Rabbi Akiva's position: neither one nor two can be mefir
Rabbi Eliezer's kal vachomer argument comparing divine designation to human acquisition
Rabbi Akiva's response: the yevamah is not fully like an arusah

macro_segments.title: Gemara's Conceptual Framework: Zika (Bond) and the Nature of the Yavam Relationship

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Akiva's position explained as denying zika—no concrete bond exists yet
Rabbi Yehoshua and Rabbi Eliezer affirm zika but dispute its application with two brothers
Rabbi Ami's ukeimta: applying the Mishnah debate to the case where one brother performed ma'amar
Ma'amar as a rabbinic act and Beis Shammai's view that ma'amar creates a kinyan gamur

macro_segments.title: Beis Shammai's Doctrine of Ma'amar: Kinyan Gamur vs. Narrow Scope

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Elazar's narrow reading of Beis Shammai: ma'amar only removes the co-wife concern
Illustration of the three-brother scenario and the sister-in-law conflict
Beis Shammai's position: ma'amar prevents the obligation to consider the other sister

macro_segments.title: The Requirement of Mezonot: Making the Yavam's Status Equivalent to a Ba'al

micro_segments.title: Beit Din obligating the yavam to pay mezonot to the yevamah
The Ran's principle: mezonot payment combined with the woman's understanding creates a ba'al status
Mezonot as the factor that elevates the relationship to nisuin and permits hafara without the father

macro_segments.title: Textual Analysis of the Mishnah and Talmudic Evidence for the Ma'amar Case

micro_segments.title: Examination of Rabbi Eliezer's kal vachomer argument in light of the assumed ma'amar scenario
Rava's question: whether ma'amar creates erusin or nisuin status
Resolution from the Mishnah: yafer without mentioning the father implies ba'al status
Rav Yitzchak's counter-position: yafer means beshutfut (together with the father)

macro_segments.title: Braiyta Evidence: Rabbi Eliezer's Kal Vachomer and Rabbi Akiva's Decisive Response

micro_segments.title: Citation of the Tosefta paralleling the Mishnah debate
Rabbi Eliezer's argument: a stranger becomes a husband; how much more so the yavam
Rabbi Akiva's distinction: a husband excludes others; the yavam does not
Rabbi Yehoshua's challenge to Rabbi Akiva on the single-brother case
Rabbi Akiva's final response: like other laws, the yevamah is not fully his wife

macro_segments.title: The Gemara's Extraction of Key Principle: Yevamah Status and Exclusion from Hafara

micro_segments.title: Demonstrating that the debate presupposes a ma'amar scenario
Confirming from the Braiyta that ba'at lereshuto refers to a post-ma'amar state
Rabbi Akiva's clinching argument: she is not subject to death by stoning, hence not eshet ish
The final Mishnah phrase restated: ein yevama gemurah l'ish confirms Rabbi Akiva's position

topical_tags.term: Beis Shammai vs. Beis Hillel (kinyan gamur through ma'amar)
Rabbi Eliezer vs. Rabbi Akiva (yavam status and hafara)
Rabbi Yehoshua (yavam authority only with single brother)
achot zekukato (sister required to the yavam)
eshet ish (married woman, with adultery implications)
Rava (questioning ma'amar status as erusin or nisuin)
Rabbi Elazar (narrow reading of Beis Shammai)
Ran (medieval commentator on mezonot and ba'al status)
Ben Azzai (commenting on Rabbi Akiva's brilliance)
Rav Yitzchak (proposing yafer means beshutfut with father)
indeterminate relationship (uncertainty about which brother has bond)
